data.description:
The completion-verification close gate's escape hatch `--force-close="<reason>"` is reachable from the CLI but NOT from the Python library API: `rebar.transition(...)` exposes only `force`, not `force_close` (`src/rebar/__init__.py:125`). A programmatic client that hits the close gate with LLM deps absent therefore has no in-library bypass and must shell out to the CLI — a real client-facing reliability/UX gap.

## Why
Library, CLI, and MCP are meant to be behaviorally at parity over one store (`tests/interfaces/facades/test_parity.py`). The close-gate bypass is an asymmetry that breaks that contract for library consumers.

## What / files
- `src/rebar/__init__.py:114-181` — add a `force_close: str | None = None` parameter to the `transition` wrapper, threaded through to the command layer.
- `src/rebar/_commands/transition.py` — accept + honor `force_close` on the close path (the CLI already maps `--force-close` here; reuse that seam, do not duplicate the gate logic).

## Scope
In: library `transition(force_close=...)` parity + a parity test. Out: changing gate semantics or the CLI surface (already correct).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `rebar.transition(id, "in_progress", "closed", force_close="reason")` closes a ticket under `require_completion_verification_for_close=true` without running the verifier, mirroring the CLI.
- [ ] The forced close records the same closed-without-signature signal as the CLI path (`signature: null`).
- [ ] A parity test asserts CLI and library force-close behave identically.
- [ ] No change to the non-forced close path.
data.title: Library/CLI parity: add force_close to rebar.transition
